description: This figure presents a comparative scatter plot analyzing the apparent densities of alloying elements in ferritic steels derived from regression modeling against pure elemental densities and literature values. The y-axis displays density in kg/m³ ranging from 0 to 16,000, while the x-axis lists individual elements: Fe, Si, Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni, V, S, and Cu. Three data series are differentiated by symbols: filled squares with error bars represent "Apparent Density, Regression" (the inverse of regression coefficients r_i with 95% probability error bars), open squares denote pure elemental "Density," and triangles indicate "Average of Apparent Density" from literature. The visualization reveals that Cu exhibits the highest apparent density (~11,800 kg/m³) followed by Mo (~10,500 kg/m³), indicating these elements increase ferrite density, whereas S shows the lowest values (~2,500 kg/m³), signifying a strong density-reducing effect. The caption states the regression model achieves R² = 0.994 with maximum deviation of 4.2 kg/m³ and average deviation of 1.4 kg/m³. The chart demonstrates close agreement between regression results and literature values for Cr, Cu, Fe, Mn, Mo, Ni, S, and Si, while V shows deviation, supporting the validity of the thermodynamic modeling approach for steel density prediction.

This figure presents a scatter plot illustrating the computed density of cementite (Fe3C) as a function of alloying element mass content, specifically for chromium (Cr) and manganese (Mn). The vertical axis shows density in kg/m³ ranging from 7000 to 7800, while the horizontal axis represents mass content from 0 to 1. Two data series are plotted: solid diamonds represent Cr-containing cementite with Pnma structure, and open triangles represent Mn-containing cementite with Pbnm structure. The data reveal a decreasing linear trend for both elements, with density dropping from approximately 7750 kg/m³ to 7600 kg/m³ for Cr (low concentration range 0–0.25) and from 7700 kg/m³ to 7080 kg/m³ for Mn (extended range 0–0.9). These calculated values correspond to Equations (6) and (7) in the text, which quantify how Cr and Mn substitution affects cementite density. The figure supports the thermodynamic modeling approach for ferritic steels by providing the cementite density input required for the regression analysis of steel density prediction.

This figure presents a regression analysis comparing the apparent density of alloying elements in austenitic steel against their theoretical elemental densities and literature values. The vertical axis displays density in kg/m³ ranging from 0 to 12000, while the horizontal axis shows various elements and phases including Fe(fcc), Cr, Cu, Si, Co, Ni, Mn, Mo, TiC, Ti, C, and N. Three data series are plotted: solid black squares representing apparent density derived from regression coefficients (1/r_i), open squares indicating theoretical elemental densities, and triangles showing average apparent density values from literature. The figure reveals that TiC, C, Cr, Mn, N, Si, and Ti reduce the apparent density of austenite (values below Fe(fcc) at ~8000 kg/m³), whereas Co, Cu, Mo, and Ni increase it. Notable deviations exist between apparent and theoretical densities for certain elements, with TiC, Ti, C, and N displaying significant uncertainty ranges (error bars). This visualization supports the development of a predictive density model for austenitic steels based on 64 batches with R²=0.975, demonstrating that solute effects on steel density deviate from simple linear mixing rules.
